core_takeaway: Agent_1 beat Agent_5 because the accepted allocation gave both their top items, but Agent_1's Quill was worth 61 while Agent_5's Jewel was worth 48; Agent_1 also anchored the Quill-defense frame and submitted the universally accepted allocation.
why_or_counterexample: Yes on outcome and agenda-setting, though Agent_5 was not excluded: Agent_5 still received Jewel, so the 13-point gap mostly reflects preference magnitudes plus Agent_1's stronger proposal alignment.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened by making Quill non-negotiable and asking for a defense pact, then recentered the table on Agent_1 as Quill defender. Agent_5 entered last, made Jewel central, and eventually endorsed the Agent_1 defender-first baseline because it still delivered Jewel.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Available for all five agents. The private notes converged on a Defender-First frame: protect Quill, satisfy Agent_3 with Stone, Agent_2 with Apple, and Agent_5 with Jewel, while managing Pencil timing.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Agent_1 proposed the efficient one-item-per-agent allocation that matches the final allocation. Agent_5's formal proposal gave Agent_5 no items. The system tabulation contains an internal inconsistency: counts show Proposal #1 at 5 accept and Proposal #5 at 0 accept, while one line says Proposal #5 was accepted.
phase_notes.private_voting: The raw private votes support Proposal #1 unanimously and reject Proposal #5 unanimously. Agent_5 accepted Proposal #1 because it gave Jewel, then rejected its own proposal because Jewel went elsewhere.
phase_notes.reflection: No reflection section is present in the transcript.

mechanisms.theme: Accepted allocation gave Agent_1 a higher-valued top item
mechanisms.explanation: The final allocation gives Agent_1 Quill and Agent_5 Jewel. Both are top priorities, but Agent_1 values Quill at 61 and Agent_5 values Jewel at 48, exactly matching the 13-point first-over-last gap.

mechanisms.theme: Agent_1 anchored the public frame around Quill protection
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 used the first speaking position to make Quill the central protected item and later converted that into a defender-first plan with Agent_1 as defender. This gave the table a stable frame that preserved Agent_1's best item.

evidence.quote: I am almost certainly aiming to keep Quill.
keeps my top priority, Quill, protected

mechanisms.theme: Agent_1's proposal was broadly acceptable while Agent_5's was not
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 submitted the allocation that matched each agent to a high-value item and received universal support in the vote counts. Agent_5's proposal allocated Agent_5 nothing and was rejected by every voter in the raw votes.

evidence.quote: Assign each item to the agent who values it most

mechanisms.theme: Agent_5 also secured its main goal, limiting a pure positional explanation
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_5 repeatedly made Jewel the central demand and accepted the baseline because it delivered Jewel. This is a counterexample to a simple story that the last speaker was shut out; Agent_5 lost only because its top-item value was lower than Agent_1's.

evidence.quote: Jewel is by far my top value (48).
I get Jewel allocated to me by Round 3
I can back the Defender-First baseline

mechanisms.theme: Private thinking converged on the same defender-first compromise
mechanisms.explanation: The private notes show agents independently treating the Agent_1 Quill-defense baseline as the robust settlement. That convergence made it easier for Agent_1's position to persist through formal voting.
